text: Last class you rewrote your chat messages to sound clear and professional.
Today you audit your email, your reports, your proposals and your chat.

Quick update: deck is ready, no rush.
Here is the draft email to the new partner. Could you take a quick look at the tone before I send it?
The content is good, but the register is too casual. It opens with Hi and reads like a chat. Raise it for a senior reader.
Understood. I will change the salutation to Dear Mr. Costa and soften the request with I was wondering if you could.
Good. And lead with the bottom line, then point to the attachment. Keep it concise, he will read it on his phone.
Got it. I will proofread it once more and end with a clear sign-off before it goes out.
Now that is a professional email. Same message, right register, right channel. Send it.
Dear Mr. Costa, I was wondering if you could help.
The full details are in the attachment.
This plan will cut your costs and save hours.
Let me proofread it once more, then I will hit send.

Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. Before that draft email to the new partner goes out, I would like you to audit it. Three quick checks. First, the register. Right now it reads like a chat message, far too casual for a senior partner. Raise it: a proper salutation, a softened request, a clear sign-off. Second, the structure. Lead with the bottom line, then point him to the attachment, and keep the whole thing concise, because he will read it on his phone. Third, the polish. Proofread it once more for typos, and double check the attachment is actually attached. The content is fine. It just needs the right tone for the right reader. Send it back to me when it is ready.

Let me think about how to pull this whole writing block together into one habit. In lesson sixteen I learned to match the register and tone of an email to the reader, formal for a partner, relaxed for a colleague. In lesson seventeen I learned to write an executive summary that leads with the bottom line. In lesson eighteen I learned to make a proposal persuasive, linking each point to a clear benefit. And in lesson nineteen I learned to keep a chat message short without losing the manners. An audit is all four at once. Before I send anything, I check the register, the structure, the case, and the channel. And the most important rule is simple. The reader, not the writer, decides whether the message worked. If it lands clearly with them, the audit passed.

First, you choose the channel and the reader the message is for. Then you set the right register, with the salutation that fits the reader. After that, you lead with the bottom line and keep the message concise. Next, you make the case persuasive, linking each point to a benefit. Finally, you proofread, add a clear sign-off, and only then send.

Always proofread for typos before you hit send.
Keep the email concise: three sentences, not three paragraphs.
